title: 36th International Kangaroo Mathematics Contest 2020 — Écolier (Grades 3-4)
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper. For personal educational practice only.

questions.question_text: A mushroom grows day by day. On which day does the mushroom look as shown on Tuesday?
questions.solution: The mushroom in the picture has a specific size reached on Friday. See PDF page 1 for the growth sequence figure.
solution_steps: Step 1: The mushroom grows each day from Monday through Friday.
Step 2: Count the growth stages shown in the figure to identify which day matches the pictured mushroom.
Step 3: The mushroom shown corresponds to Friday (Day 5).

questions.question_text: Which piece completes the pattern of orange and white tiles?
questions.solution: The missing piece must complete the alternating orange and white tile pattern. See PDF page 1 for the tile pattern figure.
solution_steps: Step 1: Examine the existing tile pattern to determine the alternating color rule.
Step 2: Identify which region is missing and what color pattern it requires.
Step 3: Only option E fits the pattern without breaking the alternating rule.

questions.question_text: In the grid below, some cells are shaded. Each shaded cell shows a calculation that equals 20. Which cells should be shaded? (Only cells whose calculation equals 20 are shaded.)

options.A: Top three cells and bottom-left cell
options.B: Top two cells and bottom two cells
options.C: All cells in top row only

questions.solution: Check each calculation: 16+4=20 ✓, 19+1=20 ✓, 28-8=20 ✓, 2×10=20 ✓, 16-4=12 ✗, 7×3=21 ✗. The four cells whose calculations equal 20 form the pattern in option A.
solution_steps: Step 1: Evaluate each cell's calculation: 16+4=20 (shade), 19+1=20 (shade), 28-8=20 (shade), 2×10=20 (shade), 16-4=12 (do not shade), 7×3=21 (do not shade).
Step 2: The shaded cells are the top three and the bottom-left cell.
Step 3: This matches option A.

questions.question_text: Which of the following figures has the largest fraction of its area shaded?
questions.solution: Compare the shaded fractions in each figure. See PDF page 2 for the figures. Option A has the largest shaded fraction.
solution_steps: Step 1: Estimate or calculate the shaded fraction for each figure.
Step 2: Compare all five fractions.
Step 3: Option A has the greatest shaded portion of its total area.

questions.question_text: Which of the following figures can be made using the given pieces (rectangle, triangles, and circles)?
questions.solution: Try to assemble each figure using the given geometric pieces. See PDF page 2 for the pieces and figures. Only option E (bicycle-like shape) can be formed from the given set of pieces.
solution_steps: Step 1: Identify the available pieces: a rectangle, triangles, and circles.
Step 2: Attempt to construct each answer figure from those pieces.
Step 3: Only option E can be assembled exactly using all the given pieces.

questions.question_text: Elli the kangaroo stands on square 1 in a 4×4 grid and jumps 3 squares forward each time. Which is the highest-numbered square she lands on?
questions.solution: Elli starts at square 1 and jumps +3 each time through the grid. Her path: 1 → 4 → 7 → 10 → 13 → 16 → 19. The next jump would go to 22, which is outside the grid, so the highest square she reaches is 19.
solution_steps: Step 1: Start at square 1 and add 3 repeatedly: 1, 4, 7, 10, 13, 16, 19.
Step 2: The next value would be 22, which exceeds the 4×4 grid (squares 1-20 or per the grid layout).
Step 3: The highest square Elli lands on is 19.

questions.question_text: A cube has animal stickers on each face. Two positions of the cube are shown. Which animal is on the face opposite to the duck?
questions.solution: By analyzing the two positions of the cube, we can determine which faces are opposite each other. The duck is opposite the fly. See PDF page 3 for the cube illustrations.
solution_steps: Step 1: From the first position, note which animals are on visible faces and their relative orientations.
Step 2: From the second position, cross-reference to identify opposite faces.
Step 3: The face opposite the duck is the fly (option E).

questions.question_text: Casper wants to cover a grid using pieces of different sizes. Each piece must be a different size. What is the maximum number of pieces he can use?
questions.solution: To maximize the number of pieces, use pieces of sizes 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 (all different). Their total area is 1+2+3+4+5=15, which matches the grid. So the maximum is 5 pieces.
solution_steps: Step 1: The grid has a fixed total area. To maximize pieces, use the smallest possible sizes.
Step 2: Use pieces of sizes 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 — all different — totaling the grid area.
Step 3: It is possible to tile the grid with 5 different-sized pieces, giving a maximum of 5.

questions.question_text: Cindy colors a target with 5 concentric rings (plus a center). The outermost ring is red. No two adjacent rings may have the same color. She uses only red and blue. How many rings are red?
questions.solution: With 6 regions (center + 5 rings) and outermost = red, the colors alternate: red, blue, red, blue, red, blue from outside to inside. Counting the red regions: ring 5 (outer), ring 3, ring 1 (inner) = 3 red regions.
solution_steps: Step 1: Label regions from outermost (6) to center (1). Region 6 is red.
Step 2: Colors must alternate: 6=red, 5=blue, 4=red, 3=blue, 2=red, 1=blue.
Step 3: Red regions are 6, 4, 2 — a total of 3 red rings.

questions.question_text: A square pyramid is placed on a table. Which of the following shows the view from directly above?
questions.solution: Looking down at a square pyramid from directly above, you see the square base with the apex in the center and four triangular edges radiating from the center to each corner. See PDF page 4 for the figure.
solution_steps: Step 1: A square pyramid has a square base and four triangular faces meeting at an apex.
Step 2: Viewed from above, the square base is visible with the apex at the center.
Step 3: Lines connect the apex to each corner of the square, creating an X pattern inside the square — this matches option C.

questions.question_text: A dog is tied to a corner of a rectangular hut that is 7 m long and 5 m wide. The leash is attached 1 m below the top-right corner, and the leash is 11 m long. Five treats are placed around the hut. How many of the five treats can the dog reach?
questions.solution: The dog is tied at the top-right corner with an 11 m leash. The leash wraps around the hut corners, reducing by the perimeter distance traveled. With 11 m total reach, the dog can access 4 of the 5 treats positioned around the hut.
solution_steps: Step 1: The dog starts with 11 m of leash at the top-right corner.
Step 2: Going along the 7 m side uses 7 m, leaving 4 m around the next corner.
Step 3: Going along the 5 m side uses 5 m, leaving 6 m around the next corner.
Step 4: By checking each treat's position relative to the dog's reachable area, 4 treats are within range.

questions.question_text: A fence is 10 m long and 2 m tall. Posts are placed every 1 m, including at both ends. Each post uses 2 vertical poles. How many poles are needed in total (vertical poles for posts plus horizontal rails at top and bottom)?
questions.solution: A 10 m fence has 11 posts (at 0 m, 1 m, 2 m, …, 10 m). Each post uses 2 vertical poles: 11 × 2 = 22. There are 2 horizontal rails (top and bottom), each spanning 10 m with 10 sections: 2 × 10 = 20. Total = 22 + 20 = 42.
solution_steps: Step 1: Count the posts: a 10 m fence with posts every 1 m has 11 posts (including both endpoints).
Step 2: Each post uses 2 vertical poles: 11 × 2 = 22 vertical poles.
Step 3: Two horizontal rails (top and bottom) each have 10 sections: 2 × 10 = 20 horizontal poles.
Step 4: Total poles = 22 + 20 = 42.

questions.question_text: A kangaroo starts at step 1 and jumps up 7 steps at a time. A rabbit starts at step 100 and jumps down 3 steps at a time. They jump at the same time. On which step are they on the same step for the first time?
questions.solution: After n jumps: kangaroo is at 1+7n, rabbit is at 100-3n. They meet when 1+7n = 100-3n → 10n = 99 → n = 9.9. Since n must be an integer, check n=9: kangaroo=64, rabbit=73. After the 10th jump: kangaroo=71, rabbit=70. They pass through step 70 simultaneously during jump 10 (rabbit descends from 73 to 70, kangaroo ascends from 64 to 71, crossing at 70). So they meet at step 70.
solution_steps: Step 1: After n simultaneous jumps, kangaroo is at 1+7n and rabbit is at 100-3n.
Step 2: Set equal: 1+7n = 100-3n → 10n = 99 → n = 9.9 (not integer).
Step 3: After jump 9: kangaroo=64, rabbit=73. After jump 10: kangaroo=71, rabbit=70.
Step 4: During jump 10, rabbit passes through step 70 (going 73→70) and kangaroo passes through step 70 (going 64→71), so they share step 70 simultaneously.

questions.question_text: Three friends each think of a number. Their numbers add up to 50. Each of them subtracts the same secret number from their number, and the three results are 24, 13, and 7. What is the smallest of the three original numbers?
questions.solution: The sum of the three results is 24+13+7=44. The original sum is 50. So 3 × secret = 50-44 = 6, meaning the secret number is 2. The original numbers are 24+2=26, 13+2=15, and 7+2=9. The smallest is 9.
solution_steps: Step 1: Sum of results = 24+13+7 = 44. Original sum = 50.
Step 2: Three friends each subtracted the secret: 50 - 3×secret = 44 → secret = 2.
Step 3: Original numbers: 24+2=26, 13+2=15, 7+2=9.
Step 4: The smallest original number is 9.

questions.question_text: A crown of 10 tokens is arranged in a circle. Each token touches two neighbors and the numbers on touching tokens must add up to the same value. What is the value of X?
questions.solution: Using the constraint that each pair of adjacent tokens sums to the same constant, work around the circle to find X. See PDF page 5 for the token values. X = 4.
solution_steps: Step 1: Identify the common sum S from a pair of known adjacent tokens.
Step 2: Use S to find unknown token values step by step around the circle.
Step 3: X = 4 satisfies all adjacency constraints.

questions.question_text: Anna has three 1 cm sticks and three 3 cm sticks. She wants to make a square. She may break sticks but not join them. Which side length square can she make?
questions.solution: A square with side 3 cm has perimeter 12 cm. Using three 3 cm sticks gives 9 cm for three sides. The fourth side of 3 cm can be made from three 1 cm sticks: 1+1+1=3 cm. Total used: three 3 cm sticks + three 1 cm sticks = exactly her stock. A 3 cm square is possible.
solution_steps: Step 1: A 3 cm square needs four sides of 3 cm each.
Step 2: Three sides use the three 3 cm sticks directly.
Step 3: The fourth side uses the three 1 cm sticks: 1+1+1=3 cm.
Step 4: All six sticks are used exactly, so a 3 cm square is achievable.

questions.question_text: A dice is rolled to the right along a track. Three faces along the path are marked with '?'. What is the sum of the numbers on those three marked faces?
questions.solution: Track the orientation of the dice as it rolls right. On a standard die, opposite faces sum to 7. By following the rolling sequence, the three '?' faces sum to 7. See PDF page 6 for the track figure.
solution_steps: Step 1: Note the starting orientation of the die from the figure.
Step 2: Roll the die rightward one step at a time, updating which face is on top, bottom, left, right, front, back.
Step 3: At each '?' position, record the face value.
Step 4: Sum the three recorded values: the total is 7.

questions.question_text: 6 ice creams are ordered: 3 vanilla, 2 chocolate, 1 lemon. There are 6 toppings: 3 cherry, 2 wafer, 1 choc chip. Each ice cream gets exactly one topping, each topping is used exactly once. Every vanilla ice cream must get a different topping. Which combination is IMPOSSIBLE?
questions.solution: The 3 vanilla ice creams must get 3 different toppings, using one of each type: 1 cherry, 1 wafer, 1 choc chip. This uses up all toppings of each type except 2 cherry remain for 2 chocolate + 1 lemon. With 2 cherry left and 3 remaining ice creams, the lemon must get a cherry topping. Therefore 'lemon with wafer' is impossible.
solution_steps: Step 1: Vanilla (×3) must have 3 different toppings → uses 1 cherry, 1 wafer, 1 choc chip.
Step 2: Remaining toppings: 2 cherry for 2 chocolate + 1 lemon (3 remaining ice creams).
Step 3: The only toppings left are 2 cherry, but there are 3 ice creams — impossible without the wafer.
Step 4: Since wafer is already used by vanilla, lemon cannot get wafer. 'Lemon with wafer' is impossible.

questions.question_text: Tom knows his wife's name and her seat. He gives three clues, each with exactly one correct element (either correct name or correct seat, but not both). Which is his wife's name and seat?
questions.solution: Testing option A (Abbey, seat 1) against each of the three clues: each clue has exactly one correct element. Abbey is correct in clue 1 (seat wrong), seat 1 is correct in clue 2 (name wrong), and Abbey is correct in clue 3 (seat wrong). All three clues check out with exactly one match.
solution_steps: Step 1: For each answer option, check it against all three clues.
Step 2: Option A (Abbey, seat 1): gives exactly one correct element per clue.
Step 3: All other options give 0 or 2 correct elements in at least one clue.
Step 4: The answer is Abbey, seat 1.

questions.question_text: Numbers 1 through 8 are placed on a board, each in a triangle, square, or circle. The sum of the numbers in triangles is 10, and the sum in squares is 20. What is the number in the circle?
questions.solution: The sum of all numbers 1 through 8 is 1+2+3+4+5+6+7+8=36. Triangles sum to 10, squares sum to 20. The circle contains 36-10-20=6.
solution_steps: Step 2: Triangle sum = 10, square sum = 20.
Step 3: Circle value = 36 - 10 - 20 = 6.

questions.question_text: A parrot has a head, tail, and wings, each colored one of 3 colors. No two parts of the same parrot have the same color. One such parrot is already made. How many more different parrots can be made?
questions.solution: The total number of ways to color 3 parts using 3 distinct colors is 3! = 6. Since one parrot is already made, there are 6-1=5 more parrots possible.
solution_steps: Step 1: The number of ways to assign 3 distinct colors to 3 parts is 3! = 6.
Step 2: One parrot is already made.
Step 3: Remaining possibilities = 6 - 1 = 5.

questions.question_text: Children form teams of 5 or 6. There are 43 children in total. How many teams are formed?
questions.solution: Let x = number of teams of 5, y = number of teams of 6. Then 5x + 6y = 43. Testing values: if y=3, then 5x=25, x=5. So 5+3=8 teams. Check: 5×5+6×3=25+18=43 ✓.
solution_steps: Step 1: Set up equation: 5x + 6y = 43.
Step 2: Try y=3: 5x = 43-18 = 25, so x=5. Both positive integers.
Step 3: Total teams = x+y = 5+3 = 8.

questions.question_text: Which of the following shapes CANNOT be cut into three different pentominoes (pieces of 5 squares each)?
questions.solution: Each shape has 15 squares (3×5). Try to partition each into three distinct pentominoes. Option B cannot be divided into three different pentominoes due to its geometry. See PDF page 7 for the shapes.
solution_steps: Step 1: Each shape consists of 15 unit squares (3 pentominoes × 5 squares each).
Step 2: Attempt to tile each shape with three different pentomino pieces.
Step 3: Options A, C, D, E can each be partitioned into three different pentominoes.
Step 4: Option B cannot be so partitioned.

questions.question_text: In the subtraction KAN - ROO + GA, each letter represents a different digit (0-9). What is the maximum possible value of the result?
questions.solution: KAN - ROO + GA = 100K+10A+N - 100R-11O + 10G+A = 100(K-R)+11A+N+10G-11O. To maximize: set K=9 (large), R=1 (small), O=0... but O appears as ROO so O being 0 gives 100R. Maximize: K=9, R=1, A=8, G=7, N=6, O=2. Result = 100(9-1)+11(8)+6+10(7)-11(2) = 800+88+6+70-22 = 942.
solution_steps: Step 1: Expand KAN-ROO+GA = 100K+10A+N - (100R+11O) + 10G+A = 100(K-R)+11A+N+10G-11O.
Step 2: Maximize by making K large (9), R small (1), A large (8), G large (7), N large (6), O small (2).
Step 3: Check all digits different: K=9, R=1, A=8, G=7, N=6, O=2 ✓ (digits 9,1,8,7,6,2 all distinct).
